title: Derived Hecke Action For Weight One Modular Forms Via Classicality
speaker: Gyujin Oh
abstract: It is known that a p-adic family of modular forms does not necessarily specialize into a classical modular form at weight one, unlike the modular forms of weight 2 or higher. We will explain how this obstruction to classicality leads to a "derived" action on modular forms of weight one, which can be understood as the so-called derived Hecke operator at p. We will also investigate the role of the derived action in the study of p-adic periods of the adjoint of the weight one modular forms.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological Hecke setting for weight one
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up modular curves, Hecke correspondences, the line bundle omega^k, and Hecke eigenspaces in H^0 and H^1, singling out k=1 because the same line bundle omega occurs in both degrees.

proof_stages.label: Archimedean Dolbeault degree shift
proof_stages.summary: Using Hodge theory and the Dolbeault complex on the complex modular curve, a weight-one eigenform gives a holomorphic H^0 class while complex conjugation of the dual form gives a corresponding H^1 class, producing a transcendental degree-shifting operation.

proof_stages.label: Stark normalization and global action
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that Stark-type algebraicity for the adjoint Artin representation should normalize the transcendental Archimedean class by a logarithm of a Stark unit, yielding a conjectural global action whose different realizations are expected to be compatible.

proof_stages.label: Away-from-p derived Hecke operator
proof_stages.summary: For primes ell not equal to p with p dividing ell minus 1, a torsion cohomology class of Gamma_0(ell) obtained from a discrete logarithm is inserted into the Hecke correspondence to define a mod-p derived Hecke operator, conjecturally matching the reduced Stark action.

proof_stages.label: p-adic overconvergent replacement for the ell=p case
proof_stages.summary: Because the naive deep-p-level analogue has bad singularities, the speaker replaces it by the geometry of ordinary loci, Igusa towers, overconvergent modular forms, and the infinite-level modular curve with its Hodge--Tate period map.

proof_stages.label: Ordinary complex and Lambda-derived p-action
proof_stages.summary: Taking N(Z_p)-invariants gives the usual U_p operator, passage to the ordinary part and higher Hida theory identifies the resulting complex with ordinary classical cohomology, and the remaining Z_p^x/Lambda action yields the ell=p derived operator compatible with log_p of the Stark unit.

proof_stages.label: Hida-family and Sen-operator reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The p-adic construction is reinterpreted through derivatives of critical p-adic families, Hida-family Galois representations, weight-one classicality, and the Sen operator, leading to the work-in-progress goal of relating the ell=p conjecture to the away-from-p conjecture.

examples.name: Higher-weight Petersson norm comparison
examples.note: An audience comparison with higher-weight modular forms explains that the transcendental normalizing factor should be viewed like a Petersson norm or L(1,Ad^0), serving only to clarify the role of the Stark regulator.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only compares the weight-one Stark regulator factor to the higher-weight Petersson norm/L(1,ad0) factor without computing or deriving such a norm in an example

passes.reason: Higher-weight Petersson norms are standard invariants used as an analogy for the weight-one Stark-regulator factor, not as a failure case.
Higher-weight Petersson norms are invoked as the standard analogous period factor, not as a structurally special object or a failure case.
The Petersson norm/L(1,ad^0) comparison is a standard higher-weight period analogy, not a structurally special object or a disproof.
A higher-weight Petersson norm comparison is a standard modular-form normalization analogy, not a structurally special object or a failure case.
A higher-weight Petersson norm is a standard period/invariant used for comparison with the weight-one Stark regulator, not a structurally special object or a refutation.
The Petersson norm for higher-weight modular forms is invoked as a standard analogous period, not to refute a claim.
Higher-weight Petersson norms are standard period invariants used only as an analogy for the weight-one Stark-regulator normalization.
Used only as an analogy for the weight-one Stark/Petersson-period factor, and higher-weight Petersson norms are structurally ordinary rather than special.
used as a standard higher-weight analogue for the Stark/Petersson period comparison, not to disprove anything
Used only as the standard higher-weight analogue where the transcendental factor is the Petersson norm.
Used only as a standard higher-weight analogy for the transcendental Petersson-period factor, with no structural extremality or exceptionality.
The higher-weight Petersson norm is invoked as the standard period/regulator analogue of the weight-one Stark unit normalization, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Ordinary p-adic L-functions as a contrast
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker contrasts the desired purely p-adic function giving log_p of the global unit with standard p-adic L-functions, whose leading terms would include unwanted L-invariant factors.
examples.engagement_reason: ordinary or actual p-adic L-functions are invoked only as a contrast to the desired purely p-adic function, with no concrete interpolation or specialization worked out

passes.reason: Ordinary p-adic L-functions form a standard systematic class, invoked to show the naive L-function explanation gives the wrong leading term.
Standard ordinary p-adic L-functions are used to show the tempting L-value interpretation gives the wrong leading term, while being structurally typical p-adic L-functions.
Ordinary p-adic L-functions are used as a failed comparison, since known interpolating L-functions give a leading term with extra factors rather than the desired log of the global unit.
Ordinary p-adic L-functions are invoked to show the naive L-value interpretation gives the wrong leading term, so they serve as a negative contrast to that claim.
Ordinary/standard p-adic L-functions are invoked to show the naive expected L-value source gives the wrong leading term, serving as a contrast to the desired purely p-adic function.
Ordinary p-adic L-functions are standard objects used here to show the naive L-function/leading-term approach gives the wrong quantity with extra factors.
Ordinary p-adic L-functions are a standard systematic family invoked to show the naive known-L-function explanation gives the wrong leading term.
Used to rule out the tempting interpretation via standard ordinary p-adic L-functions, which give the wrong leading term, while being structurally typical p-adic L-functions.
ordinary p-adic L-functions are invoked as the usual construction that fails to give the desired log-unit invariant because of extra interpolation/L-invariant issues
Used to show that ordinary known p-adic L-functions do not give the desired pure log-unit invariant.
Standard ordinary p-adic L-functions are invoked as the naive comparison that fails to give the desired log-unit quantity, rather than as structurally special objects.
Ordinary p-adic L-functions are used as a foil showing the desired log-of-unit quantity is not obtained from the usual interpolating p-adic L-functions.
